model.label: GPT-5.2
model.description: OpenAI • Smaller GPT frontier model tuned for fast, cost-efficient reasoning and tool use.

pick.rationale: Tennessee is the better overall team on both major efficiency sets (KenPom #15 / Torvik #15) and, most importantly in a 6/11, brings a much stronger defensive profile (top-15 AdjD) that should travel and make it harder for SMU’s offense to stay efficient for 40 minutes. SMU can absolutely win if its top-30 offense gets hot from three and it avoids live-ball turnovers, but its defense (around top-95) is the more exploitable unit in this matchup. With no clearly verified, matchup-flipping availability news found in the quick scan, I’m siding with Tennessee’s higher two-way floor and stop-making edge.

reasoningStep.title: Midwest 6/11: Tennessee vs SMU
reasoningStep.summary: Picked Tennessee based on a clear two-way efficiency edge driven by elite defense, while acknowledging SMU’s offense gives it a real upset path via shooting variance.
evidence: KenPom: Tennessee #15 (AdjO 121.1, AdjD 95.0) vs SMU #42 (AdjO 123.0, AdjD 104.8).
Torvik: Tennessee #15 (AdjO 121.51, AdjD 95.48) vs SMU #42 (AdjO 123.06, AdjD 104.92).
Matchup dynamic: SMU’s upset path is offense/3-point variance; Tennessee’s advantage is defensive consistency and overall adjusted margin.
Quick web/news scan did not surface a definitive late-breaking availability issue for either team that would outweigh the efficiency gap.

The Vols (22-11) are a No. 6 seed in March Madness and play the winner of a First Four game between No. 11 Miami of Ohio (31-1) and No. 11 SMU (20-13) on March 18 in Dayton, Ohio. The survivor gets Tennessee on March 20 (4:25 ET, TBS) in Philadelphia at Xfinity Mobile Arena. Here is our pick for Tennessee's first-round game against either Miami or SMU. Buy Tennessee basketball NCAA tournament tickets How do Miami (Ohio) and SMU stack up in NCAA Tournament First Four game? Miami has generated a lot of attention throughout the season. The RedHawks went an undefeated 31-0 in the regular season as it fought for an NCAA Tournament bid. It fell short in the MAC Tournament quarterfinals with a surprising 87-83 loss to UMass, squandering an opportunity to get an automatic qualifier in March Madness. USA TODAY Survivor Pool: Ready to win March Madness? Join USA TODAY's Survivor Pool Today! USA TODAY Bracket Challenge: Think you can beat our expert? Join USA TODAY's Bracket Challenge today! SMU also checked out of the ACC Tournament early. After rolling over Syracuse in the first round, the Mustangs lost 62-58 against Louisville. SMU's steals leader and third-leading scorer is former Tennessee guard B.J. Edwards. Strength of schedule favors SMU in this First Four matchup. The Mustangs had four Quad 1 wins over Louisville, North Carolina, Texas A&M and at Wake Forest. Miami played a lighter schedule and didn't have any wins over a power conference opponent. The RedHawks have four overtime wins and closed the season with three wins by two points before its tournament loss. The multiple scoring options could be difficult to guard and give the RedHawks a higher chance of pulling off an upset. modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.

modelTrace.content: {"pick":{"gameId":"r64-midwest-5","winnerId":"tennessee","confidence":0.76,"rationale":"Tennessee is the better overall team on both major efficiency sets (KenPom #15 / Torvik #15) and, most importantly in a 6/11, brings a much stronger defensive profile (top-15 AdjD) that should travel and make it harder for SMU’s offense to stay efficient for 40 minutes. SMU can absolutely win if its top-30 offense gets hot from three and it avoids live-ball turnovers, but its defense (around top-95) is the more exploitable unit in this matchup. With no clearly verified, matchup-flipping availability news found in the quick scan, I’m siding with Tennessee’s higher two-way floor and stop-making edge."},"reasoningStep":{"id":"2026-bracket-r64-midwest-5","title":"Midwest 6/11: Tennessee vs SMU","summary":"Picked Tennessee based on a clear two-way efficiency edge driven by elite defense, while acknowledging SMU’s offense gives it a real upset path via shooting variance.","evidence":["KenPom: Tennessee #15 (AdjO 121.1, AdjD 95.0) vs SMU #42 (AdjO 123.0, AdjD 104.8).","Torvik: Tennessee #15 (AdjO 121.51, AdjD 95.48) vs SMU #42 (AdjO 123.06, AdjD 104.92).","Matchup dynamic: SMU’s upset path is offense/3-point variance; Tennessee’s advantage is defensive consistency and overall adjusted margin.","Quick web/news scan did not surface a definitive late-breaking availability issue for either team that would outweigh the efficiency gap."]}}
